title: Conflux Systems - Integration compatibility discovery (SDK, gateway, quotas)
preview: Intro discovery — Conflux is evaluating Redwood for a hybrid deployment. Main concerns: network egress, VPC private endpoints, SSO/SAML, KMS integration, and preserving latency at high concurrency. They use LangChain and LlamaIndex heavily; want SDK adapter info, gateway/proxy patterns, and quota enforcement semantics. Agreed to run a short benchmark and exchange sample traces.

title: 2028-01-12 - Integration burn-in readout (Redwood Private VPC pilot)
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2028-01-12
Time: 16:00 PST
Duration: 56 minutes
Attendees: Sarah Kim (Redwood AE); Mateo Alvarez (Redwood SE); Priya Nair (Redwood CSM); Jordan Lee (AtlasMed VP Eng); Rhea Patel (AtlasMed NetOps); Samir Gupta (AtlasMed Security); Evan Cho (AtlasMed SRE)

Summary:
Quick mid-pilot burn-in readout — network/e2e telemetry, kernel upgrade patch results, and next steps for extended soak. Customer raised a small telemetry spike and two flaky handshake events during peak load; Redwood SE ran a mitigation (egress shaping + prefix cache tweak) during the call that reduced error rate. Action items and timelines agreed.

Topics: 
- Burn-in results (latency P95/P99, token throughput)
- Kernel compatibility and quantized runtime patch notes
- Networking: PSK handshake flakiness, egress shaping, MTU and path MTU discovery irregularities
- Telemetry spikes and tracing gaps
- Security: KMS integration, audit logs and SOC2 concerns
- Next steps and soak plan (50k token soak + traffic profile)

Transcript:
[00:00] Sarah Kim: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, appreciate the time. Quick roll call then Mateo will walk through the burn-in numbers.
[00:12] Jordan Lee: Yep, Jordan here. Thanks.
[00:15] Rhea Patel: Rhea on NetOps.
[00:17] Samir Gupta: Samir for security.
[00:19] Evan Cho: Evan, SRE.
[00:22] Mateo Alvarez: Cool, thanks. Alright so the high level — we ran two 6-hour soak windows last night across both the VPC interconnect and a simulated on-prem path. The P95 latency for the hosted variant was stable at ~220ms, P99 ~420ms under the synthetic mix. On your dedicated private nodes we saw P95 at 180ms, P99 at 370ms, but we got a couple of spikes during the handshakes.
[00:55] Jordan Lee: When you say spikes during handshakes, do you mean the PSK handshake or the mTLS rekey? Which one?
[00:59] Mateo Alvarez: Mostly the PSK handshake — there's an intermittent timeout at the network layer on a subset of requests that route through your east-2 egress. We traced it to a retry window that collided with our KV cache warm-up in a couple of cases.
[01:20] Rhea Patel: Yeah we saw that too, specifically when we had the burst at 3:10am — we had a thundering herd from one of our batch services. MTU looked normal but there were a couple of ICMP fragmentation responses that delayed the path.
[01:36] Mateo Alvarez: Right, so two mitigations we tested: 1) a small egress shaping rule to smooth bursts (we pushed that at 02:00) and 2) an adjustment to the KV cache prefetch window so the client doesn't block on the first request. Both reduced the handshake failures from ~0.9% to ~0.08% in the second soak.
[02:00] Jordan Lee: Nice, that reduction is meaningful. Did the egress shaping add any tail latency?
[02:07] Mateo Alvarez: Minimal — the P99 shifted maybe +10ms for that short period, but overall error budget improved.
[02:14] Samir Gupta: From a security perspective, how invasive is the shaping? Any packet inspection or header rewrite? We need to ensure nothing alters TLS/SNI.
[02:25] Mateo Alvarez: No packet inspection, it's strictly rate shaping on egress IPs — nothing touches TLS. We also documented the rules and can share the exact iptables/nft commands.
[02:36] Sarah Kim: I'll attach the snippet to the ticket after the call.
[02:40] Evan Cho: On the telemetry spikes — we noticed traces missing KV cache cold-start spans in about 12% of traces during the first soak. That made it harder to correlate.
[02:53] Mateo Alvarez: Yep, that's a tracing instrumentation gap when we hit a fallback path in the runtime — it doesn't always emit the prefetch span if we fast-fail to a fallback model. We're shipping an instrumentation patch in the kernel build that should emit a consistent span, Mateo: note to self.
[03:10] Sarah Kim: (laughs) automated note to Mateo.
[03:13] Mateo Alvarez: I'll include the trace patch in the release notes.
[03:18] Rhea Patel: About the kernel upgrade — you pushed a quant runtime that changed the kernel selection heuristics. We had one node that failed to load the quant model, any insight?
[03:30] Mateo Alvarez: Yes, that was due to an ABI mismatch with the GPU driver on that node. It's a weird edge-case where the driver exposes an extension differently and the quant loader aborted. We pushed a compatibility fallback in the patch; the node recovered after a restart. We're going to provide a checklist for driver versions that are validated.
[03:52] Jordan Lee: Will that require an upgrade on our side or can Redwood detect and automatically choose the compatible loader?
[03:58] Mateo Alvarez: We can auto-select the fallback loader when we detect the driver signature, but we recommend matching the validated driver versions if possible for deterministic perf. We'll include the recommended versions in the checklist.
[04:12] Samir Gupta: KMS question — the key rotation window we requested, can that be coordinated so tokens don't fail mid-soak? We need a maintenance window.
[04:22] Sarah Kim: Good catch. Priya, can you coordinate with Samir on rotation windows?
[04:26] Priya Nair: Yep, I'll sync after this call. Samir, what's your preferred window?
[04:31] Samir Gupta: Prefer early morning PST, ideally a 30-minute window where we can pause high-volume tests. Also need the audit trail for rotation events.
[04:40] Mateo Alvarez: Our runtime supports KMS via a transient key cache; we won't force refreshes mid-request, but if rotation happens and the client doesn't handshake, we'll log the event and surface it in the audit. We'll add rotation events to the telemetry export.
[04:55] Evan Cho: One more SRE nit — when you did the egress shaping change did you also bump the connection pool size? We saw a small regress in throughput in the 1-2 minute window.
[05:05] Mateo Alvarez: We did not change pool size; we changed the token bucket rate. The throughput regression is expected briefly while connections drain and rewarm. For the extended soak we'll try a smoother ramp to avoid that blip.
[05:18] Jordan Lee: Can you run a soak with our real traffic profile? We can sanitize and share a representative sample this afternoon.
[05:24] Sarah Kim: That would be great. Jordan, if you can share the top-10 prompt families and the traffic distribution, Mateo can load that into the harness.
[05:33] Jordan Lee: Will do.
[05:35] Rhea Patel: About the MTU/ICMP issue — our infra team will run a traceroute with DF set and forward the results. We suspect one hop in the east-2 route is doing weird fragmentation.
[05:44] Samir Gupta: From a compliance side, we need the SOC2 docs and the HSM/KMS attestation. Also a clear sign-off path if we push into production.
[05:52] Priya Nair: We'll send the SOC2 packet and KMS integration guide to Samir today.
[05:56] Sarah Kim: Ok let's wrap with specific deliverables and dates.
[06:02] Mateo Alvarez: Deliverables: kernel/instrumentation patch (in repo, DM link), egress shaping commands, extended soak plan.
[06:10] Jordan Lee: We'll send the sanitized prompt set and traffic mix by EOD tomorrow.
[06:15] Rhea Patel: We'll run the DF traceroute and send results by 2028-01-14.
[06:18] Samir Gupta: I'll review SOC2 and KMS docs and reply with any gaps by 2028-01-13.
[06:25] Sarah Kim: Great. Follow up — next sync 2028-01-19 10:00 PST to review soak outcomes. That's it, thanks everyone.
[06:33] Jordan Lee: Thanks.
[06:35] Evan Cho: Thanks all.

Post-call notes (auto):
- Telemetry spike primarily correlated to KV cache cold starts during a thundering herd event. Instrumentation patch will add a consistent prefetch span.
- Handshake failures reduced via egress shaping + cache prefetch tweak; residual <0.1%.
- One quant loader ABI mismatch caused node restart; workaround applied; checklist to be shared.
- Security: KMS rotation audit and SOC2 docs in flight.

Links referenced: 
- Kernel patch PR: (link to internal repo)
- Egress shaping snippet: (link to snippet)
- AtlasMed sanitized traffic sample (to be shared by customer)
- SOC2/KMS packet (to be shared)

End of transcript.

title: POC access orchestration + KMS handoff sync
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-07-21
Start: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~46 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Lee (Redwood AE), Mateo Alvarez (Redwood SE), Tasha Grant (CSM), Priya Nair (BlueWave Head of Platform), Liam O'Connor (Infra), Sofia Zhang (Security), Marcus Chen (ML)

Summary (auto): quick sync on onboarding, permissions, KMS handoff, and a short runbook for the first latency bench. Agreed next steps around service account and security docs.

[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for carving time. quick agenda: creds/gateway status, KMS handover, small bench run, and any blockers. Priya, you wanna kick off with current state?
[00:18] Priya: yeah thanks jordan. so, we've got staging infra mostly spun up. the k8s nodes are there, vpn tunnel to redwood gateway is in progress but we hit a firewall issue — our netops needs the final IPs. that's blocking the service account test.
[00:36] Jordan: right, we sent the ephemeral IP list last night but it's a small window — i'll resend the static endpoint. mateo, any notes about the gateway side?
[00:47] Mateo: yeah from the SE side, once the tunnel is open we can validate auth flow; two things i want to call out: first, KMS integration — redwood supports both cloud KMS and HSM via customer-managed keys; we've seen customers use GCP KMS and bring your own HSM. second, RBAC mapping for service accounts — recommend a dedicated principal for the pilot with read-only logs access.
[01:12] Liam: ok for the firewall, we need the IPs and the ephemeral port list. also, does the gateway require egress to any 3rd party? some of our policies disallow unknown outbound.
[01:23] Tasha: quick note, we have a one-pager in the drive that spells out the minimal egress rules — i'll drop the link in the notes. also, the security pack contains the allowed endpoints.
[01:35] Sofia: on KMS, we need to know key rotation cadence and audit logs. we can't have the keys exported, and we need proof that redwood's control plane doesn't ever see plaintext keys.
[01:47] Jordan: totally — redwood's private deployment model for this pilot will keep key material in your KMS, we only store key references and ciphertext. i'll attach the KMS integration diagram. mateo, can you briefly explain the KMS handshake?
[01:59] Mateo: sure. basically the agent in your VPC will call the KMS to unwrap, we never persist unwrapped keys outside the runtime memory. there's a short-lived session token for the inference container. sometimes docs call it a "key valley cache" in speech to text — ignore that, it's KV cache.
[02:18] Marcus: quick clarifying q, does the KV cache persist across restarts? we plan to warm it with prefix caching.
[02:26] Mateo: cache is in-memory per runtime; we can snapshot warm states on deploy but it's not durable across node termination unless you use our dedicated warm pool. for the pilot, i suggest enabling prefix caching and small batch sizes to start.
[02:39] Priya: batch sizing — we saw previous notes recommending 8-16 token batches depending on model. what's your baseline for latency?
[02:48] Mateo: baseline depends on model and quant. if you run the 7B quantized profile we sent, single-shot p95 should be ~120-180ms on our dedicated tier; hosted will vary. we should run a short bench with your sample trace — that will give concrete numbers.
[03:04] Sofia: any competitors or longer tail risks? anthro… anthropic came up in an internal doc as a vendor who offers private endpoints — we need to confirm differences on audit logs.
[03:15] Jordan: noted. we'll include comparative notes.

Topics discussed:
- Access gating (service account, IP allowlist)
- KMS integration and key handling
- KV/prefix caching and batch guidance
- Short benchmark plan and schedule
- Security pack and SOC2/ISO snippets

[03:40] Liam: i can push the firewall change once i have the final IPs. can you send me the JSON list not a doc? our automation consumes JSON.
[03:50] Jordan: yes will send JSON and the human-readable list.
[03:56] Tasha: also attached is the small runbook for the bench: sample payload, endpoints, and expected response shape. link: https://drive.redwood.example/poc/runbook (see security pack folder).
[04:10] Marcus: small nit — do we have example token limits for embeddings vs generation? trying to size our requests.
[04:20] Mateo: embeddings are charged per vector unit — for this pilot use 512-dims at first; generation has tokens in/out. matthew, sorry wrong name — yes generation token caps we recommended in the doc.
[04:34] Priya: haha ok — minor confusion. overall blockers are the firewall + service account. security team needs the KMS diagram and the SOC2 excerpt before greenlighting.
[04:45] Sofia: also want to run a short threat model — i'll take a pass once i see the diagrams.
[04:51] Jordan: great. recap: we'll send security pack + KMS diagram as a consolidated doc, update IPs as JSON, mateo will run bench once creds are up, and tasha will share the bench runbook link.
[05:04] Priya: timeline check — if firewall is updated by 7/26 and we run bench on 7/28, we can have a short pilot report by 7/30.
[05:12] Jordan: sounds good — set the milestone. any other issues?
[05:18] Liam: one more infra ask — do you support mTLS to the gateway or only token-based? our infra prefers mTLS.
[05:26] Mateo: we support both; mTLS is available in dedicated and private deployments. for hosted we'd recommend token + IP allowlist; for your pilot we can enable mTLS for extra assurance.
[05:40] Sofia: perfect. thanks.

[05:46] Jordan: ok we'll close. thanks everyone, i'll follow up with the packet and the ip json.

Links and artifacts referenced: https://drive.redwood.example/poc/security-pack.pdf, https://drive.redwood.example/poc/runbook, bench spreadsheet: https://sheets.redwood.example/bench/poc-bluewave

End of transcript.

title: HarbourPoint — First-call runway & hosting fit
preview: Discovery with HarbourPoint to understand inference needs for a customer-facing summarization product. Primary concerns: 99th-p latency, cost, data residency/PHI, and time-to-first-token (TTFT). Discussed hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (VPC/on-prem) tradeoffs, free credits quickstart, and next steps for a POC bench run.

title: Hemispheric routing & failover: KV cache scaling + on-prem SLA review
preview: Deep technical session: discussed a hemispheric routing approach to keep regional latency low while preserving cost; reviewed KV cache warmup and sticky routing tradeoffs; quantified batching thresholds vs p99 latency; worked through on-prem VPC handoff and audit/KMS requirements; agreed on a DR playbook draft and next engineering benchmarks.

title: Inference Capacity Staircase & Rollbacks Playbook
preview:
Header:
Date: 2026-03-18
Start: 11:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Mateo Alvarez, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor, Jordan Blake, Elena Park, Noah Wright, Sana Mir, Greg Foster

Summary:
Quick internal workshop to align on a concrete 'capacity staircase' (phased ramps), explicit rollback criteria for model and capacity regressions, and a short playbook for dry-run/drill execution. Focus was on launch readiness for the next private deployment wave and a plan to avoid cost surprises while keeping SLOs.

Topics:
- Capacity staircase definition (levels, triggers)
- Canary and dry-run plan
- Rollback criteria: quality regressions vs infra regressions
- Observability needs and runbook edits
- Security/audit notes for private deployments

Action items (captured live): see metadata section above.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: alright, uh thanks everyone for squeezing this in, I know the calendars are tight. the goal is to leave with a concrete, like, staircase thresholds doc and the first dry-run scheduled.
[00:18] Elena: yep, from exec side want clear guardrails — if latency crosses 220ms p95 for more than 5min we need an automatic step down, or at least an alert that triggers rollback.
[00:30] Noah: I think 220 as a hard number might be tough on the 8k contexts, we should scope by model size. maybe per-model thresholds, and then a global SLO budget.
[00:45] Mateo: agreed. we have telemetry split by model+region already but we need a staircase with 3 levels: baseline, stress, and surge. baseline is normal autoscale, stress is reserved burst, surge is emergency cap (manual). each level has triggers: latency, error rate, token throughput.
[01:05] Priya: can we call them v1/v2/v3? the names matter less than the actions — for v2 the plan is increase batch window slightly and enable KV caching for prefixes, for v3 we should auto-fallback to a quantized variant or smaller model.
[01:25] Liam: caution on auto-fallback — there's user-affecting quality regressions. we need rollback criteria both for infra failures and for quality regressions. that means an observable that's not just latency: e.g. eval prompt set failure rate or hallucination proxy.
[01:40] Sana: on the security side, any autoscaling or rollbacks in private deployments must be logged with the instance id and KMS key usage. audit trail is mandatory for some customers. also, VPC egress events need to be captured.
[02:00] Jordan: ok capturing, also note that cost must be part of the staircase. if burst > X token-minutes we trigger a cost review window and possibly step-down. otherwise finance will freak.
[02:15] Greg: for dry-run we should pick a low-traffic weekend slot and run a canary that ramps traffic using replay plus synthetic tails to exercise long-context paths. the sim should include KV cache warmup so we don't get skewed results.
[02:35] Ava: agreed — Mateo you own the sim plan?
[02:40] Mateo: yep, i'll do a 4-hour sim with three ramps, I'll include latency, p95/p99, tail lat, token throughput, and error rate. spreadsheet will have columns for thresholds and pass/fail criteria.
[02:55] Elena: what's the rollback mechanism we're comfortable with? full traffic cut or model variant fallback?
[03:05] Priya: prefer staged: first fallback model variant (quantized), if QA metric drops below threshold then step to capacity step-down (move traffic to smaller region or dedicated pool), last resort cut traffic. incorporate vote from oncall.
[03:25] Liam: that needs clear criteria: e.g., quality proxy > 2% fail on eval set OR latency p99 > 500ms for 10 minutes leads to vote. we should automate the vote if it's infra-exit (OOMs, GPU errors).
[03:45] Noah: on capacity accounting: the staircase should expose reserved burst buckets per customer so we can bill and also stop accidental overrun. we should include a strap on the console that shows current staircase stage and remaining burst minutes.
[04:05] Priya: we talked about KV cache being mis-heard in prior calls as 'KV cash' — note: caching warms cost but helps latency; we'll measure cost per token with cache on/off.
[04:20] Jordan: heh yep, recorded.
[04:25] Ava: operational playbook edits — Liam, can you propose amendments to runbook for staged rollback, including who has the unilateral authority to move to v2/v3?
[04:40] Liam: i'll propose that oncall can move to v2 (stress) but v3 (surge) requires approval from capacity lead + product (Noah + Elena). final traffic cut needs VP ok.
[04:55] Greg: and we'll add a checklist for the dry-run: prechecks (metrics sane), smoke test endpoints, KMS audit hook enabled, and roll-forward plan.
[05:10] Ava: timeline check: draft stairs doc by Monday (Ava), sim by Wednesday (Mateo), rollback hook prototype by next Friday (Priya), dry-run targeted end of next week (Greg).
[05:25] Elena: quick question — how do we handle private deployments where customers want full manual control? do we still enforce staircases?
[05:35] Sana: for privacy customers we ship the scaffolding; by default it's opt-in for automatic steps, but we still require audit logs and a manual approval path. the defaults will be conservative.
[05:50] Mateo: technical gotcha — when we do model variant fallback we need to ensure KV cache format compatibility or else cache miss storms will happen. we should add a cache compatibility check in the rollback hook.
[06:05] Priya: yep, that's on my prototype scope. i'll include a quick latency-safe migration that invalidates prefixes older than N seconds to avoid cold-miss avalanche.
[06:25] Jordan: can we add a short 'blast radius' diagram to the staircase doc that maps components affected (frontend, router, runtime, kv cache, billing)?
[06:40] Ava: good idea, Jordan — that'll help execs understand risk.
[06:50] (crosstalk)
[07:00] Ava: ok smaller notes: include specific SLO windows, escalation path, and the exact telemetry dashboards we'll need. Mateo, please list dashboard IDs so we can pair them to thresholds.
[07:18] Mateo: will do — i'm also going to include an 'emergency capacity token' concept that is separate from reserved burst and requires manual approval.
[07:30] Noah: one more ask — instrument the console to show last rollback reason: quality/infrastructure/cost. that's critical for postmortems.
[07:45] Priya: agreed. building that into trace logs is straightforward but we need UX work to surface it. I'll coordinate with Jordan for the wire.
[07:58] Ava: wrapping — any final blockers?
[08:05] Elena: none here. just want the doc to be crisp.
[08:10] Liam: need to validate SSO and audit hooks for private customers before dry-run. Sana, can you sign off once done?
[08:20] Sana: yes i'll sign off once I see KMS audit logs and VPC egress hooks in place.
[08:30] Ava: cool. recap next steps in chat and i'll own the doc.
[08:35] (meeting ends)

Post-call quick notes (auto):
- Some speech mis-recognition around 'KV cache' and 'canary sim' replaced with 'KV cash' and 'candy sim' in earlier auto transcript; corrected above.
- A few timestamps drift because of overlapping talkers; manual cleanup recommended if used for legal/audit purposes.

Recap (final):
- Deliverables: staircase thresholds doc (Ava), canary sim results spreadsheet (Mateo), rollback hook prototype (Priya), dry-run schedule and checklist (Greg), runbook edits (Liam).
- Dry-run target: weekend slot week of 2026-03-29.
- Acceptance criteria: per-model thresholds pass in sim, KV cache compatibility verified, security audit hooks present, and rollback behavior tested with postmortem template filled.

References shared during call:
- \"staircase-spec-v0\" doc link (to be attached in calendar)
- canary-spreadsheet template (sheet link)
- runbook PR template (repo link)

End of transcript.
